These 1959 standards were part of a collection of voluntary U.S. grade standards issued by the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)[1](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/06/United_States_standards_for_grades_of_processed_fruits%2C_vegetables%2C_and_certain_other_products_%28as_of_July_2%2C_1959%29_%28IA_unitedstatesstan14unit_4%29.pdf), [2](https://www.scribd.com/document/806254807/Dairy-plant), [3](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/GOVPUB-A-PURL-gpo30568/pdf/GOVPUB-A-PURL-gpo30568.pdf).

The document from 1959 explicitly states that it is an "Historic, archived document" and advises not to "assume content reflects current scientific knowledge, policies, or practices," indicating its outdated status[1](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/06/United_States_standards_for_grades_of_processed_fruits%2C_vegetables%2C_and_certain_other_products_%28as_of_July_2%2C_1959%29_%28IA_unitedstatesstan14unit_4%29.pdf), [2](https://www.scribd.com/document/806254807/Dairy-plant).

Specific examples demonstrate this supersession:
*   The first issue of the United States Standards for Grades of Dehydrated (Low Moisture) Peaches, which was in effect since November 30, 1959, was superseded by an amended issue effective June 30, 1974[4](https://www.ams.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media/Dehydrated_Peaches_Standard%5B1%5D.pdf).
*   United States Standards for Grades of Dehydrated (Low Moisture) Prunes became effective on June 13, 1960[5](https://www.ams.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media/Dehydrated_Prunes_Standard%5B1%5D.pdf).

As of August 2023, the USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) continues to develop and maintain U.S. grade standards for fruits, vegetables, and specialty crops. These standards are voluntary and aim to provide a uniform language for describing the quality and condition of commodities in the marketplace[6](https://www.ams.usda.gov/grades-standards/fruits). The AMS regularly reviews and updates these standards, with PDF publications on their website superseding any previously issued publications[6](https://www.ams.usda.gov/grades-standards/fruits). In some instances, outdated or unnecessary voluntary grade standards have been removed from the Code of Federal Regulations, though the standards may still be administered and cataloged by the AMS Specialty Crops Inspection (SCI) Division[7](https://www.ams.usda.gov/rules-regulations/removal-us-grade-standards). This ongoing process ensures that the standards reflect current marketing practices and industry needs[8](https://www.ams.usda.gov/rules-regulations/us-standards-grades-fresh-fruits-and-vegetables-fruits-and-vegetables-processing).

The percentage of United States standards for grades of processed fruits, vegetables, and certain other products listed as dehydrated that have been superseded by a new version since July 2, 1959, as of August 2023 is effectively **100%**.
